reference: GATE CS — Compiler Design / Backend Phases

map.condition: Alias created when two formals refer to same location
map.use: \text{Call-by-Reference: aliasing possible when same variable passed twice}

map.condition: Expression re-evaluated each time parameter is used
Formal copied back to actual on procedure exit
map.use: \text{Call-by-Value-Result: copy-in at call, copy-out on return}

map.condition: Variable resolved in lexically enclosing scope
map.use: \text{Static Scope: use access link / display to find enclosing frame}

map.condition: Variable resolved in most-recently-called active scope
map.use: \text{Dynamic Scope: walk call stack to find binding}

formulas.name: Call-by-Value
formulas.latex: \text{formal} \leftarrow \text{copy of actual at call time; changes to formal do NOT affect actual}
formulas.plain: formal = copy of actual; callee's changes are local only
formulas.whenToUse: Simplest mechanism — actual is never modified by callee; use when you see local copies.

terms.means: parameter variable inside the procedure
argument expression at the call site

formulas.trap: Passing a pointer by value still lets the callee modify the pointed-to data — the pointer itself is copied, not the object.

formulas.name: Call-by-Reference
formulas.plain: formal is an alias for the actual location; P(x,x) makes two formals alias the same cell
formulas.whenToUse: When two actuals are the same variable or overlap in memory; used to detect aliasing problems.

terms.means: memory address / location of the actual
two formals pointing to the same memory cell

formulas.trap: Call-by-reference and call-by-value give different results when the same variable is passed more than once (e.g. P(a, a)).

formulas.name: Call-by-Value-Result (Copy-in Copy-out)
formulas.plain: Copy actual into formal at call; copy formal back into actual on return
formulas.whenToUse: Simulates call-by-reference without aliasing; copy-back happens only once on exit.

terms.means: formal receives value of actual at call time
actual receives final value of formal on return

formulas.trap: If actual is an array element A[i] and i changes during the call, the copy-back goes to the location of i at call time, not at return time (different from reference).

formulas.name: Call-by-Name (Textual Substitution / Thunk)
formulas.latex: \text{Each use of formal substitutes the actual expression (re-evaluated with caller's scope)}
formulas.plain: Formal is replaced by the actual text; expression re-evaluated every access (thunk closure)
formulas.whenToUse: When an expression (not a variable) is passed and its value may change between uses.

terms.means: closure encoding the actual expression for lazy re-evaluation
Algol 60 rule: textually replace formal with actual text

formulas.trap: Call-by-name and call-by-reference differ when an array element A[i] is passed and i is modified inside the procedure — name re-indexes, reference keeps the original address.

formulas.name: When Mechanisms Differ
formulas.latex: \text{CBR} \neq \text{CBN when: (a) expr passed, (b) array elem with index change, (c) same var twice}
formulas.plain: CBR != CBN when: expression passed, or A[i] with i changing, or same variable passed twice
formulas.whenToUse: Quick check to decide if two mechanisms produce different results for a given call.

terms.means: Call-by-Reference
Call-by-Name

formulas.trap: Passing a simple variable (not an array element, not an expression) by reference and by name always give the same result — a common distractor.

formulas.name: Activation Record (Stack Frame) Layout
formulas.latex: \begin{array}{|c|}\hline \text{Return value} \\ \hline \text{Actual parameters} \\ \hline \text{Control link (saved fp)} \\ \hline \text{Access link (static link)} \\ \hline \text{Saved machine registers} \\ \hline \text{Local variables} \\ \hline \text{Temporaries} \\ \hline\end{array}
formulas.plain: [Return value | Parameters | Control link | Access link | Saved regs | Locals | Temporaries]
formulas.whenToUse: Drawing or reading an activation record in any GATE question involving calling sequences.

terms.means: pointer to caller's activation record (dynamic link / saved fp)
pointer to the enclosing scope's activation record (static link)
frame pointer — base of current activation record

formulas.trap: Control link tracks the call chain (dynamic); access link tracks lexical nesting (static). Confusing them is a classic GATE mistake.

formulas.name: Static Allocation & Recursion
formulas.latex: \text{Static allocation} \Rightarrow \text{one frame per procedure globally} \Rightarrow \text{recursion FAILS}
formulas.plain: With static allocation there is only one activation record per procedure; recursion requires multiple simultaneous frames, so it cannot work.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether recursion works with static/global allocation of variables.

terms.means: each variable has a fixed compile-time address
a procedure calling itself, needing multiple live activation records

formulas.trap: A language CAN have recursion with stack (dynamic) allocation even if local variables are allocated statically for non-recursive calls.

formulas.name: Calling Sequence Steps
formulas.latex: \text{Caller: } \text{push args} \to \text{save regs} \to \text{push return addr} \to \text{jump callee}\\\text{Callee prologue: } \text{save fp} \to fp \leftarrow sp \to sp \leftarrow sp - \text{frame\_size}
formulas.plain: Caller: push args, save regs, push return address, jump. Callee: save old fp, set fp=sp, allocate locals by moving sp.
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing an activation tree or computing the state of the stack during a procedure call sequence.

terms.means: stack pointer — top of the runtime stack
space needed for locals and temporaries of callee

formulas.trap: Return value slot is typically allocated by the CALLER before the call, not the callee.

formulas.name: Activation Tree
formulas.latex: \text{Node: activation of procedure } P\quad \text{Child: activation called by } P\quad \text{Execution order = DFS left-to-right}
formulas.plain: Activation tree: each node is one call; children are calls made during that activation; DFS order matches execution order.
formulas.whenToUse: Reconstructing the call sequence diagram (e.g. p -> s -> q -> r -> q) from a program.

terms.means: one invocation of a procedure from entry to return
depth-first search — mirrors the LIFO stack discipline

formulas.trap: Recursive calls appear as multiple nodes in the tree even though it is the same procedure — each invocation is a separate node.

formulas.name: Static (Lexical) Scope
formulas.latex: \text{Variable resolved in the textually enclosing block at the definition site; resolved at compile time}
formulas.plain: Variable binding determined by lexical (textual) nesting; same result every call regardless of who called it.
formulas.whenToUse: When the question says 'static scope rules' or asks what value a variable has under static scoping.

terms.means: nearest surrounding block that declares the variable in the source text
runtime mechanism to walk the static chain to reach the enclosing scope's frame

formulas.trap: Under static scope, a nested procedure uses the variable from its DEFINITION context, not from the procedure that called it.

formulas.name: Dynamic Scope
formulas.latex: \text{Variable resolved by searching the call stack at runtime; most recent active binding wins}
formulas.plain: Variable binding found by walking up the CALL CHAIN at runtime; whoever called most recently and declared it wins.
formulas.whenToUse: When the question says 'dynamic scope rules' or asks what the program prints under dynamic scoping.

terms.means: stack of currently active activation records
innermost activation on the stack that declares the variable

formulas.trap: Under dynamic scope, the same procedure call can give a different value for a free variable depending on WHO called it.

formulas.name: When Static and Dynamic Scope Differ
formulas.latex: \text{Static} \neq \text{Dynamic when a free variable is declared in both the definition and the calling scope}
formulas.plain: Static and dynamic scope give different results only when a variable is free in a procedure and declared differently in its textual parent vs. its calling parent.
formulas.whenToUse: Deciding which scope rule to trace for a program with nested procedures and non-local variable access.

terms.means: variable used in a procedure but not declared locally in it

formulas.trap: If a variable is locally declared in every procedure that uses it, static and dynamic scope produce the same result.

formulas.name: Block-Structured Scope (Nested Blocks)
formulas.latex: \text{Inner block variable shadows outer block variable; exits scope at end of block (}\}\text{)}
formulas.plain: A variable declared in an inner block shadows any same-named variable in an outer block; scope ends at block close.
formulas.whenToUse: When tracing programs with nested begin...end or { } blocks and same-named variables at different levels.

terms.means: inner declaration hides outer declaration of same name within its scope
region of program text where a declaration is visible

formulas.trap: After the inner block ends, the outer variable is visible again — the inner one is gone, not reassigned.

formulas.name: Runtime Memory Segments
formulas.plain: Code | Static/Global | Heap (grows up) ... Stack (grows down) — standard four-segment model
formulas.whenToUse: Any question about where variables live: local -> stack, global/static -> data segment, malloc/new -> heap.

terms.means: region for dynamically allocated objects (malloc, new)
region for activation records; grows towards lower addresses

formulas.trap: Stack and heap can collide if both grow toward each other with no guard — a classic runtime error not caught by the compiler.

formulas.name: Heap vs Stack Allocation
formulas.latex: \text{Stack: LIFO, compile-time size known, auto reclaimed}\quad\text{Heap: arbitrary lifetime, fragmentation possible}
formulas.plain: Stack: LIFO, size fixed at compile time, freed automatically on return. Heap: dynamic size, programmer-managed lifetime.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked why certain objects must be heap-allocated (closures, objects with unknown lifetime, dynamic arrays).

terms.means: Last-In First-Out — stack frames are freed in reverse creation order
heap memory wasted due to interleaved alloc/free cycles

formulas.trap: A closure (function + environment) escaping its defining scope cannot live on the stack — it must be heap-allocated.
